text: welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery today we're going to be talking about elements of an operation i'm going to be focusing on etep ventral hernia and the critical steps of planning how you dock and crossover the case we're presenting is a sixty-two-year-old man who presented with morbid obesity and medication-induced lipodystrophy he had a recurrent and incarcerated ventral hernia I had worked with this patient for many, many months to try and optimize weight, but unfortunately, because of other factors, BMI could not be reduced below forty-four. And I felt that a minimally invasive approach to hernia repair would be in the best interest of the patient for both short-term and long-term recovery potential.

So what is ETAP? It's Enhanced View Totally Extraperitoneal Access, much like to have access for amoral hernias, ETEP takes advantage of being able to perform a complex operation outside of the visceral cavity. And for abdominal wall reconstruction ventral hernia repair, we're working in the retromuscular space, anteriorly finding the rectus muscle, posteriorly the rectus sheath. ETEP has been shown to allow us to do extraperitoneal placement of mesh, develop a unified plane spanning the entire abdominal wall, and can help minimize the need for circumferential fixation. It really has taken off over the course of the last five to ten years.

Whenever planning any abdominal operation, a ventral hernia repair, it's really important to review your cross-sectional imaging, evaluating defect size, hernia contents, evidence of prior mesh, evidence of any metallic fixation, and then specifically for patients who are undergoing a possible ETEP approach looking at your rectus with your integrity of the abdominal wall, and then understanding what your potential component separation is. So this patient had very favorable anatomy, very wide rectus, over a twelve to fourteen centimeters, had a untouched upper abdomen, and then had a peri-umbilical EHSM-III hernia with a five centimeter width on the neck and then about a three to four centimeter width a large volume of incarcerated omentum and a small loop of bowel involved in this hernia, I felt that they would be a great candidate for ETEP.

And so for a lot of my trainings, whenever they're preparing for abdominal wall surgery, I really recommend setting yourself up for success by doing what I call abdominal wall mapping. Looking at your axial and sagittal views on your ct can help you review the abdominal wall understand if they have what the hernia is understand what the diastasis is if there is one and then really plan out how you're going to approach this the size of the mess you may need and where you're going to dock

The surgical setup for the E-TEP approach involves basic laparoscopic equipment for initial access, followed by robotic instruments including a force bipolar, monopolar scissors, and a mega suture cut needle driver. The patient's arms are tucked as shown in the referenced image from Dr. Bilyansky's chapter in the Atlas of Robotic General Surgery.

The surgeon uses a KII applied medical trocar for initial retromuscular access, targeting a central rectus port. The optical entry technique is employed, with the hands flattened to facilitate entry through the subcutaneous tissue and anterior sheath to reach the rectus muscle.

The surgeon's hands are parallel to the floor as insufflation begins. The trocar tip is initially stuck to the posterior sheath, but is pulled back to enter the areolar plane between the rectus muscle and the posterior sheath, initiating retro-rectus dissection.

The surgeon begins the dissection on the left side, using blunt dissection techniques to avoid vascular injuries. The focus is on medial dissection, followed by lateral dissection towards the semilunar line. Three robotic trocars are strategically placed medial to the semilunar line for optimal access during the procedure.

The surgeon places the first robotic trocar medial to the semilunar line and begins developing the surgical space using basic laparoscopic equipment. Electrocautery is employed to maintain a bloodless field, with a Maryland dissector and Hunter grasper used for tissue manipulation.

The surgeon efficiently develops the extraperitoneal space during the initial entry site using the E-TEP approach, aiming to create a large working area to minimize the risk of complications such as inadvertent perforation of the hernia sac.

The surgeon bluntly dissects areolar fibers in the retromuscular space around the hernia site, using energy devices to control potential bleeding. Visible atypical scarring patterns are present due to prior surgeries, including possible trans-fascial fixation of mesh. The anatomy reveals a wide retro rectus space, facilitating the placement of the second trocar.

The surgeon transitions the camera to a newly placed trocar and continues developing space for the third and final robotic port. The initial entry trocar remains centrally placed in the rectus, not used for robotic instruments unless optimally positioned.

The surgeon visualizes the transversus abdominis fibers through the posterior lamella of the internal abdominal oblique, indicating the initiation point for a top-down TAR approach. A spacious area has been developed, and the final trocar is placed in the upper abdomen. The robotic docking is prepared for crossover, utilizing the natural preperitoneal fat planes, including the subxiphoid fat, falciform ligament, and umbilical ligaments.

The surgeon initiates crossover by incising medially on the posterior sheath, preserving the linea alba and maintaining a one-centimeter fascia margin on the ipsilateral side. Upon entering the preperitoneal space, the contralateral rectus sheath is identified and incised to access the contralateral retro-rectus space. The dissection progresses towards the contralateral semilunar line, developing the space further.

The surgeon creates a one-centimeter cuff of fascia and begins the crossover by incising the posterior sheath. The posterior sheath is released, allowing entry into the preperitoneal fat pad. The retro rectus and retromastoid spaces are covered entirely with mesh, ensuring comprehensive coverage. The patient's anatomy is noted to be narrow, without diastasis.

The surgeon begins retromuscular dissection in the upper abdomen, encountering a small medial perforator. Hemostasis is achieved to maintain a dry surgical field, facilitating the placement of a large mesh. The dissection progresses down the abdominal wall towards the hernia site.

So there we are, an ETEP docking and crossover. I tried to do minimal editing here to just give a full sense of how this is done and for trainees how to prepare for this operation to really make that first part very educational. So ETEP ventral hernia, obviously special pre-operative planning important to have a thorough understanding of the dogma wall anatomy and then patient selection is really critical especially early in the learning curve thank you very much and i hope you enjoyed this presentation
